Regulation, hormonal axes, and clinical disorders
Total Ca²⁺
8.5–10.5 mg/dL
Total Pi
2.5–4.5 mg/dL
PTH Normal

PLO 1 — Forms, Normal Levels & Clinical Symptoms

Professor LO
PLO 1

Forms of Ca²⁺ and Pi in the body; normal blood levels; symptoms of hypo/hypercalcemia; daily dietary requirements

Calcium in the body

  • Most abundant cation in the body — found mostly in bone.
  • Functions: mineralization of bones & teeth, intracellular signaling, synaptic transmission, muscle contraction, cell membrane stability, blood clotting.

Three fractions of serum Ca²⁺

Fraction~%Notes
Free ionized Ca²⁺~50%Only biologically active form; regulated by PTH
Bound to albumin~40%Not active; affected by albumin levels & pH
Complexed to anions (esp. phosphate)~10%Not active
⚠️ Key Clinical Point: Total serum Ca²⁺ does NOT always reflect free ionized Ca²⁺. Hypoalbuminemia decreases total Ca²⁺ without true hypocalcemia — PTH keeps free Ca²⁺ normal.

Normal Blood Levels

8.5–10.5
Total serum Ca²⁺ (mg/dL)
2.5–4.5
Total serum Pi (mg/dL)
10–65
PTH (pg/mL)

Dietary Requirements (per day, adults)

  • ~1.0 g/day dietary Ca²⁺ to maintain balance (only ~40% absorbed by SI; rest in feces)
  • ~0.7 g/day dietary Pi (~65% absorbed)
  • Most foods are rich in Pi → Pi is easier to meet; calcium is the challenging one
  • ~98% of filtered Ca²⁺ and Pi is normally reabsorbed by the kidneys

Phosphate (Pi) — Forms & Importance

  • Most common forms: HPO₄²⁻ and H₂PO₄⁻
  • Functions: bone/teeth mineralization, ATP formation, second messengers, phospholipids, nucleic acid structure

Clinical Symptoms

Hypocalcemia (<8.5 mg/dL)Hypercalcemia (>10.5 mg/dL)
Tetany, muscle spasms, convulsions, seizures, paresthesia, arrhythmias Kidney stones, bone loss, hypercalciuria, organ calcification (heart, kidneys, blood vessels)

Slide 3: Ca²⁺ & Pi in Health

Slide 5: Whole-body Ca²⁺ & Pi Balance

⚖️

PLO 2 — Effect of Albumin & Blood pH on Ca²⁺

Professor LO
PLO 2

How serum albumin and blood pH affect total and free calcium

Albumin & Calcium

  • Albumin is an acidic protein → cations (H⁺ and Ca²⁺) compete for binding sites.
  • Hypoalbuminemia → less Ca²⁺ bound → ↓ total Ca²⁺, but free Ca²⁺ normal (PTH compensates)
  • Hyperalbuminemia → more Ca²⁺ bound → ↑ total Ca²⁺, but free Ca²⁺ normal
📌 Total serum Ca²⁺ changes with albumin, but free ionized Ca²⁺ is what matters clinically and is maintained by PTH feedback.

Blood pH & Free Ca²⁺

pH ChangeH⁺ / Albumin bindingFree Ca²⁺Result
Acidosis (↓ pH, ↑ H⁺) Albumin binds MORE H⁺, LESS Ca²⁺ ↑ Free Ca²⁺ → Hypercalcemia
Alkalosis (↑ pH, ↓ H⁺) Albumin binds LESS H⁺, MORE Ca²⁺ ↓ Free Ca²⁺ → Hypocalcemia (tetany!)
🏃 Classic Exam Scenario: Hyperventilation → respiratory alkalosis → ↓ free Ca²⁺ → tetany. Total Ca²⁺ unchanged!

Slide 4: Blood pH Effect on Calcium Distribution

🔬

PLO 3 — Parathyroid Hormone (PTH)

Professor LO
PLO 3

PTH: definition, structure, site of synthesis, mechanism of action

Structure & Synthesis

  • PTH = 84 amino acid peptide hormone (vital for life)
  • Synthesized by chief cells (principal cells) of the parathyroid glands as a pre-prohormone → cleaved twice → mature PTH (84 aa)
  • Secretion is pulsatile; half-life in blood = ~3–5 minutes
  • Broken down by kidney & liver into peptide fragments (some retain PTH activity)
  • Normal blood level: 10–65 pg/mL

PTH Receptor & Mechanism of Action

  • Primary receptor: PTH1R — most common; found in bone and kidney
  • Also PTH2R exists but is less significant here
  • PTH1R is a G-protein coupled receptor (GPCR)
  • PTH1R also binds PTHrP (parathyroid hormone-related peptide)
💊 PTHrP: Produced by placenta, fetal parathyroids, and some cancers. Has PTH-like effects. Most common humoral cause of hypercalcemia of malignancy.

Slide 6: PTH Structure & Parathyroids

☀️

PLO 4 — Calcitriol (Biologically Active Vitamin D₃)

Professor LO
PLO 4

Calcitriol: definition, structure, nature as a steroid hormone

  • Calcitriol = 1,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol (also: 1,25-dihydroxy vitamin D₃)
  • = the biologically active form of precursor vitamin D₃ (cholecalciferol)
  • Calcitriol is a steroid hormone → acts via intracellular receptor (not surface GPCR)

Mechanism of Action

Step 1 — Blood transport
In blood, >85% of calcitriol is bound to vitamin D binding protein (VDBP)
↓
Step 2 — Cell entry
Calcitriol dissociates from VDBP → diffuses into target cells
↓
Step 3 — Receptor binding
Binds intracellular VDR (vitamin D receptor / calcitriol receptor)
↓
Step 4 — Heterodimerization
VDR + RXR (retinoid-X receptor) form a complex that enters the nucleus
↓
Step 5 — Transcription
Complex binds VDRE (vitamin D response elements) → regulates gene transcription

Slide 14: Calcitriol Structure & Mechanism

🎯

PLO 5 — Target Sites of PTH & Calcitriol

Professor LO
PLO 5

Sites targeted by PTH and calcitriol that regulate calcium and phosphate balance

🚨 MUST MEMORIZE: Main targets of PTH = BONE and KIDNEYS. Main targets of calcitriol = SMALL INTESTINE and PARATHYROID GLANDS
HormoneTarget OrganKey Effect
PTH Bone ↑ bone resorption → ↑ Ca²⁺ AND Pi in blood (most important effect)
Kidney (proximal tubule) ↑ 1α-hydroxylase → ↑ calcitriol production; ↑ Ca²⁺ reabsorption; ↓ Pi reabsorption
Kidney (distal tubule) ↑ TRPV5/6 luminal Ca²⁺ channels → ↑ Ca²⁺ uptake into blood
Calcitriol Small intestine ★ #1 effect ↑ absorption of dietary Ca²⁺ (and Pi) via transporters and calbindin
Parathyroid glands Feedback inhibition of PTH production (chief cells)
Bone Synergizes with PTH → ↑ RANKL, ↓ OPG → ↑ bone resorption
Kidney ↑ Ca²⁺ and Pi transporters; ↑ calbindin-D28K → ↑ Ca²⁺ reabsorption
🔁 Negative Feedback on PTH: PTH secretion is inhibited by ↑ free Ca²⁺ AND/OR ↑ calcitriol — NOT by elevated PTH itself. Calcitriol is the hormonal brake.

Slide 7: PTH & Calcitriol Working Together

🦋

PLO 6 — Calcitonin

Professor LO
PLO 6

Calcitonin: synthesis site, stimulation, effects on plasma calcium

  • Calcitonin = calcium-lowering peptide hormone
  • Synthesized by: C cells (parafollicular cells) of the thyroid gland
  • Stimulated by: hypercalcemia
  • Effect: inhibits bone resorption by ↓ osteoclast activity → ↓ blood Ca²⁺
  • Relatively minor effect in humans (contrast with fish/birds where it's more significant)
💊 Clinical use: Calcitonin mimetics (e.g., salmon calcitonin) are used to treat hypercalcemia and osteoporosis in certain conditions, though it is a relatively weak treatment in humans.

Slide 19: Bone Resorption — RANKL/OPG & Calcitonin

📡

CLO 1 — CaSR & Negative Feedback Regulation of PTH

Class LO
CLO 1

Calcium sensing receptor (CaSR) role in PTH production/secretion; negative feedback mechanism

The Calcium Sensing Receptor (CaSR)

  • Located on chief cells of the parathyroid glands
  • Acts as the "calciostat" — maintains free Ca²⁺ within narrow limits
  • The set point for PTH secretion = 1.1–1.2 mmol/L free Ca²⁺ (≈ 4.4–4.8 mg/mL); varies per person
  • Slight changes above/below set point → changes in PTH within minutes; blood Ca²⁺ restored within 1–2 hours

CaSR Signaling — Two States

StateTriggerSignalingEffect on PTH
Relaxed state ↓ Free Ca²⁺ (below set point) CaSR activates adenylate cyclase → ↑ cAMP ↑ PTH production & secretion
Activated state ↑ Free Ca²⁺ (above set point) CaSR activates phospholipase C → inhibits adenylate cyclase → ↓ cAMP ↓ PTH production & secretion

Negative Feedback on PTH

  • PTH is inhibited by: (1) ↑ free Ca²⁺ AND (2) ↑ calcitriol
  • In primary hyperparathyroidism (PHPT): the CaSR set-point curve shifts right → more Ca²⁺ required to inhibit PTH
💊 Calcimimetics (e.g., Cinacalcet): Drugs that activate the CaSR → mimic high Ca²⁺ → ↓ PTH production. Used in primary hyperparathyroidism and SHPT in CKD.

Slide 12: CaSR Set-Point Curve

Slide 13: CaSR Signaling Mechanism

↔️

CLO 2 — Reciprocal Relationship: Ca²⁺ vs Pi Reabsorption

Class LO
CLO 2

Reciprocal relationship between plasma calcium and phosphate reabsorption stimulated by PTH

🔁 Key Concept: PTH increases Ca²⁺ reabsorption but simultaneously decreases Pi reabsorption in the kidney → phosphaturia (Pi wasting in urine).
  • As PTH ↑ → blood Ca²⁺ ↑ (via renal reabsorption and bone resorption)
  • Bone resorption releases both Ca²⁺ AND Pi → normally counteracted by PTH-induced renal Pi excretion
  • In health: increased blood Pi from bone resorption OR dietary absorption (calcitriol) is offset by increased renal Pi excretion
  • This keeps blood Ca²⁺ and Pi in reciprocal balance
⚠️ The reason PTH promotes renal Pi wasting: if Pi accumulated along with Ca²⁺, it would complex with Ca²⁺ → calcium phosphate precipitates → ↓ free Ca²⁺. PTH-driven phosphaturia protects free Ca²⁺.

Slide 8: Reciprocal Ca²⁺-Pi Relationship

🌞

CLO 3 — Calcitriol Synthesis & 25-Hydroxy Stability

Class LO
CLO 3

Significance of stable 25-hydroxycholecalciferol; synthesis of calcitriol; regulation of production

Prevalence of Deficiency

  • ~25% of US adults are vitamin D₃ deficient; ~50% are borderline deficient

Synthesis Pathway

Precursor
7-dehydrocholesterol (provitamin D, a cholesterol derivative) — in keratinocytes
↓ UV light (skin keratinocytes)
Step 1
Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D₃) — also consumed as dietary vitamin D₃
↓ 25-hydroxylase (liver)
Step 2
25-hydroxycholecalciferol — the stable plasma marker of vitamin D₃ status
↓ 1α-hydroxylase (proximal tubule, kidney)
Step 3
1,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol = CALCITRIOL (biologically active)
↓ 24-hydroxylase (kidney) — INACTIVATION
Inactivation
24,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol (inactive)

Regulation of Calcitriol Production

Enzyme↑ Production by↓ Production by
1α-hydroxylase (→ calcitriol) PTH Calcitriol (self-inhibition; negative feedback)
24-hydroxylase (→ inactive) Calcitriol and Ca²⁺ PTH

Why 25-hydroxycholecalciferol is the clinical marker

  • Calcitriol itself has a short half-life in blood
  • 25-hydroxycholecalciferol is stably expressed in plasma → used as the standard barometer of vitamin D₃ status in clinical labs

Slide 15: Calcitriol Synthesis & Physiological Effects

Slide 16: Calcitriol Synthesis Pathway Diagram

🧪

CLO 4 — Renal Ca²⁺ Reabsorption & SI Absorption Mechanisms

Class LO
CLO 4

Mechanisms of PTH/calcitriol in renal Ca²⁺ reabsorption; calcitriol-stimulated Ca²⁺ and Pi absorption in SI

Renal Ca²⁺ Reabsorption

  • ~70% of filtered Ca²⁺ is passively reabsorbed in the proximal tubule (same for Pi)
  • Fine-tuned regulation occurs in the distal tubule

Distal Tubule — PTH & Calcitriol Effects

HormoneActionMechanism
PTH ↑ luminal Ca²⁺ channels ↑ TRPV5/TRPV6 → ↑ Ca²⁺ uptake from tubule lumen into cell
Calcitriol ↑ intracellular Ca²⁺ binding proteins ↑ Calbindin-D28K → shuttles Ca²⁺ across cell; ↑ basolateral transporters (NCX1, PMCA1b)
PTH (proximal tubule) ↓ Pi reabsorption Stimulates degradation of Pi transporters in proximal tubule → ↑ urinary Pi excretion

Small Intestine (SI) — Calcitriol-Stimulated Ca²⁺ Absorption

🚨 Most important effect of calcitriol: stimulation of dietary Ca²⁺ absorption in the small intestine!
  • Calcitriol stimulates:
    • Formation of luminal Ca²⁺ channels (TRPV5/TRPV6)
    • Production of calbindin (intracellular transporter)
    • Basolateral Ca²⁺ transporters (NCX1, PMCA1b)
  • Dietary Pi is readily absorbed by passive transport — doesn't require calcitriol to the same degree
  • High dietary Ca²⁺ can also lead to some passive paracellular absorption

Slide 9: Distal Tubule Ca²⁺ Reabsorption Mechanism

Slide 17: SI Ca²⁺ Absorption by Calcitriol

🦷

CLO 5 — Bone Resorption by PTH & Calcitriol

Class LO
CLO 5

Mechanisms by which PTH and calcitriol regulate bone resorption under physiological conditions

The RANKL/OPG System

  • Osteoblasts produce:
    • RANKL (Receptor Activator of NF-κB Ligand) — promotes osteoclast formation
    • OPG (Osteoprotegerin) — decoy receptor for RANKL; inhibits osteoclast formation
  • PTH + calcitriol synergistically:
    • ↑ RANKL production
    • ↓ OPG production

Osteoclast Activation Pathway

↑ PTH + ↑ Calcitriol → ↑ RANKL + ↓ OPG (from osteoblasts)
↓
RANKL binds RANK on preosteoclasts
↓
Preosteoclasts → mature Osteoclasts
↓
Osteoclasts dissolve hydroxyapatite → release Ca²⁺ AND Pi into blood
📌 OPG inhibition by PTH/calcitriol enhances the effect of RANKL — less decoy receptor means more RANK engagement on preosteoclasts → even more osteoclast formation. This is a cooperative amplification.

Slide 19: RANKL/OPG System — Bone Resorption Mechanism

⚗️

CLO 6 — Regulation of Plasma Inorganic Phosphate & FGF23

Class LO
CLO 6

Factors regulating plasma inorganic phosphate under physiological and pathophysiological conditions

Normal Pi Balance

  • Total blood Pi: 2.5–4.5 mg/dL
  • Most foods are rich in Pi → easily absorbed in SI
  • Under normal conditions, Pi balance is maintained primarily by renal excretion

Hyperphosphatemia — Causes

  • CKD — ↓ GFR → ↓ Pi excretion (most common cause)
  • RBC hemolysis (releases intracellular Pi)
  • Metabolic or respiratory acidosis (pulls Pi out of cells)
  • Overuse of Pi-containing clinical solutions

FGF23 — Fibroblast Growth Factor 23

🔁 New Axis: Pi balance is regulated by PTH, calcitriol, AND FGF23
  • Secreted by osteocytes in response to hyperphosphatemia
FGF23 EffectMechanismNet Result
↓ Renal Pi reabsorption ↓ Pi transporters in kidney ↑ urinary Pi excretion
↓ 1α-hydroxylase Inhibits enzyme in proximal tubule ↓ calcitriol formation

Cascade in CKD / Secondary Hyperparathyroidism (SHPT)

Trigger
CKD → ↓ GFR → Hyperphosphatemia
↓
↑ Pi → activates CaSR → ↑ PTH secretion
+ ↑ Pi → osteocytes secrete FGF23
↓
FGF23 inhibits 1α-hydroxylase → ↓ calcitriol
↓
↓ Calcitriol → less feedback inhibition of PTH → further ↑ PTH (SHPT)
↓
↑ PTH → ↑ bone resorption (hypercalcemia risk); ↓ calcitriol → ↓ dietary Ca²⁺ absorption (hypocalcemia risk)
+ Ca²⁺ + Pi form calcium phosphate → precipitates in tissues (prevents hypercalcemia)
⚠️ SHPT in CKD: Despite elevated PTH, total serum Ca²⁺ may be low to normal because: (1) ↓ calcitriol → ↓ intestinal Ca²⁺ absorption; (2) Ca²⁺ combines with excess Pi → tissue calcium phosphate deposits → prevents hypercalcemia.

Slide 20: Pi Balance & FGF23

Slide 21: Summary of Hyperphosphatemia in SHPT

🏥

CLO 7 — Hypo/Hypercalcemia: Causes & Integrated Response

Class LO
CLO 7

Common causes of hypo/hypercalcemia; integrated response to hypocalcemia

Hypercalcemia — Causes

CauseMechanismNotes
Primary Hyperparathyroidism (PHPT) Benign adenoma in one or more parathyroids → excess PTH Most common cause; elevated serum PTH is key diagnostic; CaSR set-point shifted right
Malignancy (PTHrP) Tumors secrete PTHrP → acts like PTH Most common humoral cause of hypercalcemia of malignancy
Ca²⁺ / Vitamin D supplement abuse Excess intake overwhelms regulation —
Secondary Hyperparathyroidism (SHPT) Elevated PTH from external stimulus (hypocalcemia, CKD) See CLO 6

PHPT Phenotypes

  • Asymptomatic (>80%) — mostly postmenopausal women; found on routine blood work
  • Symptomatic — kidney stones, bone loss, hypercalciuria, organ calcification (heart, kidneys, blood vessels)

Hypocalcemia — Causes

  • Vitamin D deficiency and/or insufficient dietary calcium
  • Hypoparathyroidism — parathyroid damage after thyroidectomy OR autoimmune disease
  • Chronic kidney disease (↓ calcitriol)
  • Malabsorptive GI conditions

Integrated Response to Hypocalcemia

🎯 Total serum Ca²⁺ <8.5 mg/dL → hypocalcemia → PTH system activates
Trigger
↓ Free Ca²⁺ → CaSR in parathyroids → ↑ PTH secretion (within minutes)
↓
1 — Bone
PTH → ↑ bone resorption → ↑ Ca²⁺ AND Pi released into blood
Most rapid and most important effect
↓
2 — Kidney
PTH → ↑ 1α-hydroxylase → ↑ calcitriol production
PTH → ↑ Ca²⁺ reabsorption (TRPV5/6 upregulation in distal tubule)
PTH → ↓ Pi reabsorption (phosphaturia)
↓
3 — Small Intestine
Calcitriol → ↑ dietary Ca²⁺ absorption (takes hours to days — slower effect)
↑ Pi absorption also occurs but balanced by renal Pi excretion
↓
Result
↑ Blood Ca²⁺ → CaSR activated → feedback inhibition of PTH → homeostasis restored
💡 Pi management during hypocalcemia correction: As bone resorption increases Pi in addition to Ca²⁺, PTH-driven renal phosphaturia prevents hyperphosphatemia. Increased intestinal Pi absorption is similarly buffered by ↑ renal Pi excretion.

Slide 11: Disorders of Calcium Balance

Slide 18: Integrated Hypocalcemia Response
